name: Monterrubio de la Serena and Hinojosa del Duque Loop
name_original: Camino Mozarabe: Monterrubio de la Serena - Hinojos del Duque — circular por la región de La Serena
description: Cycle the Camino Mozarabe from Monterrubio de la Serena to Hinojos del Duque, a 62.3-mile route through dehesa and olive groves.
region_intro: Road cycling around Monterrubio De La Serena features routes through the plains of La Serena, characterized by rolling agricultural landscapes and scattered hills. The region offers varied terrain, from longer, flatter sections ideal for endurance riding to routes incorporating moderate climbs. Cyclists can expect to traverse open countryside, passing through small towns and alongside reservoirs. Best road cycling routes around Monterrubio De La Serena The most popular road cycling route is Monterrubio de la Serena from Bellacázar — circular via Cabeza del Buey, a 55.0 miles (88.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 46 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers a substantial distance through the local landscape. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is La Serena: Quintana, Zalamea and Esparragosa de la Serena — circular from Castuera, a moderate 31.9 miles (51.3 km) path. This route connects several towns within the La Serena region, providing a mix of cultural and natural scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Monterrubio de la Serena and Hinojosa del Duque Loop, a 62.3 miles (100.3 km) trail leading through the broader countryside, often completed in about 3 hours 28 minutes. Road cycling around Monterrubio De La Serena is defined by extensive plains, rolling hills, and agricultural areas. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 20 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Monterrubio De La Serena's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What are the typical difficulty levels for road cycling routes around Monterrubio De La Serena?
A: The road cycling routes in Monterrubio De La Serena primarily feature moderate and difficult grades. Out of the five routes highlighted, four are considered moderate, and one is difficult, offering options for riders with varying experience levels.

Q: Are there long-distance road cycling routes available in the Monterrubio De La Serena region?
A: Yes, the region offers several long-distance routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993796462/monterrubio-de-la-serena-and-hinojosa-del-duque-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monterrubio de la Serena and Hinojosa del Duque Loop&lt;/a&gt; covers over 100 kilometers (62 miles), providing a substantial ride through the broader countryside.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Monterrubio De La Serena?
A: Given the region's climate, spring and autumn are generally the most pleasant times for road cycling in Monterrubio De La Serena. Temperatures are milder, and the landscape is often at its most vibrant. Summer can be very hot, making early morning or late afternoon rides preferable.

Q: What kind of scenery can I expect while road cycling around Monterrubio De La Serena?
A: You can expect to traverse the extensive plains of La Serena, characterized by rolling agricultural landscapes and scattered hills. Routes often pass through open countryside, small towns, and alongside reservoirs, offering a mix of natural and cultural scenery.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Monterrubio De La Serena?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are designed as loops. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1016973219/monterrubio-de-la-serena-from-bellacazar-circular-via-cabeza-del-buey" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monterrubio de la Serena from Bellacázar — circular via Cabeza del Buey&lt;/a&gt;, which is a 88.5 km (55.0 miles) circular trail.

Q: What attractions can I see along the road cycling routes in this region?
A: The routes often pass by significant landmarks. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24527487" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Cerro Masatrigo – Capilla and its Castle loop from Puebla de Alcocer&lt;/a&gt; route offers views of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839713" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro Masatrigo&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5460742" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Puebla de Alcocer&lt;/a&gt;. You might also encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5422272" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Serena Dam&lt;/a&gt; and various charming towns.

Q: Where do road cycling routes typically start in Monterrubio De La Serena?
A: Routes often start from or pass through key towns in the region. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993806019/la-serena-quintana-zalamea-and-esparragosa-de-la-serena-circular-from-castuera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Serena: Quintana, Zalamea and Esparragosa de la Serena — circular from Castuera&lt;/a&gt; route begins from Castuera, connecting several towns within the La Serena region.

Q: Are there any routes that offer particularly scenic viewpoints?
A: Yes, the varied terrain includes rolling hills that provide elevated perspectives. The route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24527487" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Cerro Masatrigo – Capilla and its Castle loop from Puebla de Alcocer&lt;/a&gt; is specifically named for its scenic views, including the prominent &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839713" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro Masatrigo&lt;/a&gt;, which is a notable natural monument.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Monterrubio De La Serena?
A: Road cyclists frequently appreciate the extensive plains and rolling hills that define the region, offering both endurance challenges and picturesque rides through agricultural areas. The network provides diverse options for exploring the local landscape.

Q: How many road cycling routes are featured in this guide for Monterrubio De La Serena?
A: This guide highlights five distinct road cycling routes around Monterrubio De La Serena, offering a curated selection of the region's best rides.

Q: Can I find routes that connect multiple towns in the La Serena region?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993806019/la-serena-quintana-zalamea-and-esparragosa-de-la-serena-circular-from-castuera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Serena: Quintana, Zalamea and Esparragosa de la Serena — circular from Castuera&lt;/a&gt; route is an excellent example, connecting several towns and providing a comprehensive tour of the area's cultural and natural scenery.
tips: The Camino Mozárabe is an old path of more than 25 km that went from Monterrubio de la Serena to Hinojos del Duque and where it is currently signposted. Unfortunately, it cannot be done with a road bike, but it crosses it several times and where you can see almost the same landscapes.
Hinojosa del Duque is a municipality in the province of Córdoba in the region of Valle de los Pedroches. Population known for Holy Week and its processions.
Road that shows us the typical Dehesa of Extremadura. Open spaces interspersed with arable fields, oak forests and areas in between with livestock and green areas. Beautiful!

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for road bikes?
faqs.answer: While the route is listed for racebikes, it's important to note that this segment is primarily part of the historic Camino Mozárabe walking pilgrimage. It features a mix of surfaces, including asphalt, dirt tracks, and unpaved paths. There's even a natural ford across the Zújar river. For a comfortable road cycling experience, you will likely need to adapt your route to stay on paved alternatives, bypassing some sections of the official pilgrimage trail.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied. You'll find paved sections, especially within and approaching towns. However, the official Camino path includes 'pistas de tierra y asfalto' (dirt tracks and asphalt), unpaved paths, and a natural ford. If you choose to follow the exact pilgrimage route, expect challenging conditions for a traditional road bike. Sticking to parallel narrow roads with minimal traffic is advisable for a smoother ride.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect to cycle through the rural landscapes of Andalusia and Extremadura. The route offers views of 'dehesa' (holm oak pastures) and cultivated fields. You'll also see the Zújar River and its meander, with the hermitage of the Virgen de la Alcantarilla being a notable landmark. The landscape transitions as you move between the two autonomous communities, providing pastoral views and a sense of solitude.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: Key points of interest include Crossing the Mozárabe route itself, which is a historic pilgrimage path. You'll pass through the settlement of Hinojosa del Duque, known for its historical architecture like the church of San Juan Bautista. Near Monterrubio de la Serena, you can see the church of Nuestra Señora de la Consolación and the hermitage of the Virgen de la Alcantarilla. The Road from Peraleda del Zaucejo to Monterrubio de la Serena is also a notable section.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during spring (March to May) or autumn (September to November) to avoid the extreme heat of summer and potential heavy rains in winter. During rainy seasons, the natural ford across the Zújar river could be difficult or impossible to cross, making paved alternatives even more critical.

faqs.question: Where does the route start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: This is a circular route connecting Monterrubio de la Serena and Hinojos del Duque. You can start in either town. Both Monterrubio de la Serena and Hinojosa del Duque are small towns, and you should be able to find parking in or near the town centers. Specific parking areas are not detailed, but local street parking is usually available.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It traverses public roads and paths.

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route passes through rural areas, it is primarily designed for human-powered travel. If you bring a dog, ensure it is well-behaved and kept on a leash, especially when passing through towns, near livestock, or on shared paths. Be prepared to carry water for your dog, as natural water sources might not always be accessible or safe.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay overnight near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, both Monterrubio de la Serena and Hinojosa del Duque are towns with amenities. You can find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options in both settlements. These towns serve as good points for rest stops or overnight stays if you plan to extend your trip.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. Most notably, it is a segment of the historic Camino Mozárabe. Other official trails that briefly overlap or are nearby include the Transandalus, Camino del Condado de Belalcázar - Etapa 1, and Vereda del Zújar.

faqs.question: What should I consider if I want to stick to paved roads?
faqs.answer: If your priority is to stick to paved surfaces for road cycling, you will need to deviate from the exact pilgrimage path in certain sections. Specifically, be aware of the dirt tracks and the natural ford across the Zújar river. It is often more comfortable to follow narrow, low-traffic paved roads that run parallel to or bypass these challenging sections, especially during or after rain.
